[step tt:"What is Binary Search?"]
Binary search finds an item in a sorted list by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half.
[callout t:info]
Works only on sorted data. Time complexity: O(log n).
[/callout]
[code]
// Example: find 7 in [2, 5, 7, 12, 19]
// Start: low=0, high=4, mid=2 -> value 7 found!
[/code]
[/step]
[step tt:"How It Works"]
Compare target to middle element. If equal, done. If target is smaller, search left half. If larger, search right half.
[callout t:inf
results.output_text:
[card]
[steps]
[step tt:"What is Binary Search?"]
Binary search finds an item in a sorted list by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half.
[callout t:info]
Works only on sorted data. Time complexity: O(log n).
[/callout]
[code]
// Example: find 7 in [2, 5, 7, 12, 19]
// Start: low=0, high=4, mid=2 -> value 7 found!
[/code]
[/step]
[step tt:"How It Works"]
Compare target to middle element. If equal, done. If target is smaller, search left half. If larger, search right half.
[callout t:info]
Each step eliminates half the remaining elements.
[/callout]
[quick-reply items:"Search left half|Search right half|Found it"]
[/step]
[step tt:"Try It Yourself"]
Find the number 9 in this sorted list: [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13]. What is the first middle index?
[quick-reply items:"Index 2 (value 5)|Index 3 (value 7)|Index 4 (value 9)"]
[/step]
[/steps]
[/card]

results.output_text:
[card]
[steps]
[step tt:"What is Binary Search?"]
Binary search finds an item in a sorted list by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half.
[callout t:info]
Works only on sorted data. Time complexity: O(log n).
[/callout]
[code]
// Example: find 7 in [2, 5, 7, 12, 19]
left = 0, right = 4, mid = 2 (value 7) -> found!
[/code]
[/step]
[step tt:"How It Works"]
Start with left and right pointers. Compare middle element to target. If equal, done. If target is smaller, search left half. If larger, search right half. Repeat until found or pointers cross.
[callout t:info]
Each step eliminates half the remaining elements.
[/callout]
[/step]
[step tt:"Try It Yourself"]
Given sorted list (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11), find 9.
[quick-reply items:"Check middle (5) then right half|Check middle (7) then right half|Check middle (9) found"]
[/step]
[/steps]
[/card]

Start with the middle element. If it matches, done. If target is smaller, search left half; if larger, search right half. Repeat.

results.output_text:
[card]
[steps]
[step tt:"What is Binary Search?"]
Binary search finds an item in a sorted list by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half.
[callout t:info]
Works only on sorted data. Time complexity: O(log n).
[/callout]
[/step]
[step tt:"How It Works"]
Start with the middle element. If it matches, done. If target is smaller, search left half; if larger, search right half. Repeat.
[code]
arr = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9]
target = 5
# middle index = 2, value = 5 -> found!
[/code]
[/step]
[step tt:"Try It Yourself"]
Which step comes first in binary search?
[quick-reply items:"Check middle element|Sort the array|Search left half|Search right half"]
[/step]
[/steps]
[/card]

results.output_text:
[think]The user asks about recursion. I'll explain the core concept: a function calling itself with a smaller problem, plus a base case. I'll use a simple factorial example and suggest tracing it. Include a source reference.[/think]
[bubble role:ai][p]Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself to solve a smaller version of the same problem. Every recursive function needs two parts: a base case that stops the recursion, and a recursive case that moves toward the base case.[/p][p]For example, factorial(n) = n * factorial(n-1), with factorial(0) = 1 as the base case.[/p][source n:1 tt:"Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs" sn:"Classic CS text on recursion" u:#][/bubble]
